data.title: Federal Reserve is expected to keep rates unchanged ahead of Fed chair transition
data.description:
As the Federal Open Market Committee meets on Tuesday and Wednesday to decide on monetary policy, the interest rate decision will be the most expected item on the list of Federal Reserve watchers’ items of interest. 

The central bank decision is being made as Fed Chair Jerome Powell helms his last meeting with Fed Chair nominee Kevin Warsh waiting in the wings. President Donald Trump continues to pressure the Fed head to cut rates.  And the FOMC will be struggling to assess whether the oil shock triggered by the Iran conflict will bleed into underlying inflation trends.

The question looming over the meeting will be whether Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell will serve out his term as a Fed governor after his term as chair ends. Now that the Department of Justice has dropped its investigation of Powell’s handling of the Fed’s renovation project, former Fed Governor Warsh is poised to be confirmed as Fed chair by the time Powell’s chair term ends on May 15. 

Powell can serve two more years as a board governor, though, a move that would break with the tradition of leaving the board after a chair’s term expires if he decides to stay.

“The most important part of this meeting is: What is Jay Powell going to do?  And what hints he’s going to give as to whether he stays on,” said Heather Long, chief economist at Navy Federal Credit Union, in an interview with Seeking Alpha. “It seems likely that he will stay on as a Fed governor, at least for a while.”

She’s most interested in Powell’s take on where the investigation stands. “The DOJ has dropped it, but he used those terms that he wouldn’t leave until it was fully over, and it’s not fully over,” Long said. “It’s just in somebody else’s hands.”

After the last FOMC meeting, Powell said, "I have no intention of leaving" the board until the Department of Justice investigation is "well and truly over." US Attorney for the District of Columbia Jeanine Pirro said she reserves the right to restart the DOJ investigations if the facts warrant it. That could leave enough doubt in Powell’s mind to hold onto his Fed governor slot. And the investigation hasn’t so much resolved as shifted to another overseer. The Inspector General of the Federal Reserve is taking over the probe to ascertain whether the Fed chair managed the project properly and whether his testimony to Congress violated any laws.

Regarding the interest rate decision, traders are pricing in a 100% probability of no change to the current 3.50%-3.75% federal funds rate range, according to the CME FedWatch tool. Even more telling, the market is pricing in no rate change for the rest of the year, compared with the start of 2026, when traders were pricing in two 25 basis-point rate cuts.

Navy Federal’s Long now expects that the central bank will reduce its benchmark rate at its December meeting. That contrasts with expectations before the Iran war, when traders were pricing in a rate cut in June or July. That hope has now vanished as the conflict led to surging oil prices.

As always, traders, economists, and other Fed watchers will be focused on any forward guidance the FOMC may provide in its statement. In the minutes for the March meeting, there was some debate on whether its statement should include language that the next rate move could be either a cut or a hike.

While it’s possible there could be more discussion of that at this meeting, Long doesn’t expect it to be in the statement. “From what I'm reading and hearing, I think the bar is very, very high, and that they would not be positioning the Fed well if they went with that language,” she said.

One reason to keep the federal funds rate target rate unchanged is the economic uncertainty. Most Fed officials have said they're in no rush to cut rates until they have a clearer picture of how the Iran war will affect inflation. With long-term inflation expectations remaining well anchored, there's no urgent need to adjust rates. Another major reason is the upcoming succession. "You don't want to rock the boat before you're handing the Fed over to the new chair," Long said.

And it's important to Powell that he hand the Fed over to his successor with policy in a "good place." "I think he's built up a lot of trust in the markets and how he passes the baton," Long said.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Oil rally accelerates on UAE OPEC exit, and these 10 energy stocks have taken notice
data.description:
Energy stocks led U.S. equities higher Tuesday, emerging as the top-performing segment in the S&P 500 (SP500) after a sharp move in crude prices.

Oil (CL1:COM) climbed 3.5% to trade just below the $100-per-barrel mark, reflecting renewed supply uncertainty. The catalyst came from the United Arab Emirates’ decision to exit OPEC, a development that has stirred concerns about cohesion within the producer group and future output coordination.

Investors interpreted the move as potentially supportive of tighter global supply conditions, lifting sentiment across the oil and gas space and driving broad-based gains among exploration, production, and midstream companies. 

Outlined below are the top 10 performing S&P 500 energy stocks on Tuesday: 

  1. Coterra Energy (CTRA), +2.9%.
  2. Occidental Petroleum (OXY), +2.9%.
  3. ONEOK (OKE), +2.9%.
  4. Targa Resources (TRGP), +2.8%.
  5. Devon Energy (DVN), +2.8%.
  6. Kinder Morgan (KMI), +2.7%. 
  7. Exxon Mobil (XOM), +2.5%. 
  8. Chevron (CVX), +2.4%. 
  9. EQT Corporation (EQT), +2.3%.
  10. Williams Companies (WMB), +2.3%. 

Energy ETFs: (XLE), (VDE), (XOP), (OIH), (AMLP), and (IXC). 

Oil ETFs: (USO), (UCO), (DBO), (OILK), and (USL).

Natural Gas ETFs: (UNG), (BOIL), and (UNL).

data.title: Apple offers developers new way to make App Store subscriptions more affordable
data.description:
• Apple (AAPL) on Monday announced a new way for developers to make their App Store subscriptions more affordable.
  • The new option allows for developers to offer monthly subscriptions with a 12-month commitment. “People can cancel their subscription at any time, which will prevent the subscription from renewing after they’ve completed their agreed-to payments to fulfill their commitment,” Apple said on its website.
  • The new type of subscription will be available to developers around the world, except for the U.S. and Singapore, Apple added. It is available to users who have iOS 26.4, iPadOS 26.4, macOS Tahoe 26.4, tvOS 26.4, and visionOS 26.4, or later, installed on their devices.
  • The release of iOS 26.5, iPadOS 26.5, macOS Tahoe 26.5, tvOS 26.5, and visionOS 26.5 is set to happen next month.

data.title: Coca-Cola posts best quarter in years, putting a spotlight on staples ETFs
data.description:
Coca-Cola (KO) shares climbed more than 6% on Tuesday after the beverage giant delivered a first-quarter earnings report that comfortably beat expectations across nearly every key metric, and the move higher rippled through the broader consumer staples space, lifting rivals PepsiCo (PEP) and Keurig Dr Pepper (KDP) as well.

For ETF investors, Coca-Cola's strong quarter adds a tailwind to consumer staples funds across the board.

Below is a list of ETFs with exposure to Coca-Cola, primarily within the consumer staples sector. The list is arranged by their Year-To-Date performance.

The list is topped by ProShares Ultra Consumer Staples ETF (UGE), with a YTD performance of 10.63%. Global X PureCap MSCI Consumer Staples ETF (GXPS) and iShares S&P 500 Cnsmr Staples Sctr UCITS ETF USD A (IMSBF) follow closely behind. Fidelity MSCI Consumer Staples Index ETF (FSTA) and Vanguard Consumer Staples Index Fund ETF (VDC) round out the top five.

The list also includes broader funds such as Invesco Dow Jones Industrial Average Div ETF (DJD) and niche offerings like First Trust Nasdaq Food & Beverage ETF (FTXG). Several income-focused ETFs appear toward the bottom of the rankings, including FT Vest DJIA Dogs 10 Target Income ETF (DOGG) and Harvest US Equity Leaders Income ETF - Class U (HBF.U:CA).

Here is the list:

  1. ProShares Ultra Consumer Staples ETF (UGE), YTD perf: 10.63%, Quant rating: Strong Sell (1.36)

  2. Global X PureCap MSCI Consumer Staples ETF (GXPS), YTD perf: 8.74%, Quant rating: Not Covered

  3. iShares S&P 500 Cnsmr Staples Sctr UCITS ETF USD A (IMSBF), YTD perf: 8.25%, Quant rating: Sell (2.22)

  4. Fidelity MSCI Consumer Staples Index ETF (FSTA), YTD perf: 7.38%, Quant rating: Sell (2.31)

  5. Vanguard Consumer Staples Index Fund ETF (VDC), YTD perf: 7.33%, Quant rating: Sell (2.32)

  6. State Street Consumer Staples Sel Sect SPDR ETF (XLP), YTD perf: 6.00%, Quant rating: Sell (2.24)

  7. Invesco Dow Jones Industrial Average Div ETF (DJD), YTD perf: 4.92%, Quant rating: Hold (2.79)

  8. iShares US Consumer Staples ETF (IYK), YTD perf: 4.77%, Quant rating: Sell (1.78)

  9. First Trust Nasdaq Food & Beverage ETF (FTXG), YTD perf: 4.64%, Quant rating: Strong Sell (1.26)

  10. FT Vest DJIA Dogs 10 Target Income ETF (DOGG), YTD perf: 2.12%, Quant rating: Sell (2.09)

data.title: Crypto investor Novogratz does not see Bitcoin crossing $100K in 2026
data.description:
Famed crypto investor Michael Novogratz does not think Bitcoin (BTC-USD) will cross $100K this year.

Bitcoin had a pretty severe sell-off all the way to $60K, but bounced up to $80K, said the Galaxy Digital CEO during the company's Q1 earnings call.

"I don't see big clean exploding in the near term," he said. "I think you're going to have some wood to chop through 80, 85. Once you get through that, the next stop is 100."

"And if you break that, then it's all price discovery. And so it's not my prediction that we break that 100 this year," said Novogratz.

Novogratz's Galaxy Digital focuses on investments in cryptocurrency, providing digital assets and data center infrastructure.

Today, Galaxy Digital posted a Q1 non-GAAP EPS of -$0.49, which beats the analyst estimates by $0.44, and revenue of $10.04B (-22.7% Y/Y), which beats by $1.29B.

However, the stock prices were little changed as concerns lingered over depreciation of digital asset prices.

BTC crossed the $100K mark in October 2025, but has been on a downward trajectory since then.

The CEO said for Bitcoin to retake $100K, "You're going to need a few things to happen."

"Mostly that will be an easing central bank. And given the war in Iraq, we've got some pretty ugly inflation prints that are going to come through the pipeline," said Novogratz.

Bitcoin-backed ETFs: (IBIT), (ARKB), (GBTC), (BRRR), (BTCO), (HODL), (BTCW), (FBTC), (BITB), and (EZBC).

data.title: Smithfield forecasts $1.1B-$1.2B packaged meats profit in 2026 while Nathan’s Famous deal shifts to 2H 2026
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Smithfield Foods (SFD) Q1 2026

Management view

  • CEO Shane Smith reported “record first quarter adjusted operating profit of $339 million and adjusted operating profit margin of 8.9%,” and said results reflected “disciplined execution of our long-term strategies, particularly in packaged meats,” while emphasizing the “benefits of our vertically integrated model.”
  • Smith highlighted Packaged Meats operating profit of $275 million on sales of $2.1 billion, and said performance included unit and share gains in higher-margin categories, citing cooked dinner sausage units up 9% and dry sausage units up 10%; he also said branded distribution points increased 5.5% and noted Packaged Meats retail lunch meat volume grew 11.1% while “the industry was down 6.5%.”
  • Smith said the company is managing macro volatility from the Middle East conflict through “pricing and mix, disciplined spending, productivity initiatives, hedging and contract and procurement actions,” and framed consumer demand as value-focused: “The U.S. consumer continues to be cautious.”
  • Smith updated two major strategic items: “Our anticipated time line to close the transaction is now in the second half of 2026 due to the impact of the partial government shutdown on statutory deadlines for the CFIUS review process,” and on Sioux Falls, “We look forward to sharing more information once we have secured final approvals.”
  • CFO Mark Hall said, “we're off to a strong start in 2026,” and emphasized capital allocation priorities: “our strong balance sheet and cash flow give us the financial flexibility to invest in growth, pay a competitive dividend and ultimately create value for our shareholders.”

Outlook

  • Hall said, “We're reaffirming the guidance we provided on March 24,” and added, “we have multiple levers we can pull in the near term, including price and mix, disciplined spending, productivity initiatives, hedging and contract and procurement actions to protect performance and keep us agile.”
  • In Q&A, Hall described quarterly phasing and segment cadence, saying Packaged Meats underlying performance is expected to be “broadly similar to the first quarter,” but with a “tougher” year-over-year comparison in Q2 due to holiday timing; he added Fresh Pork is typically “softer than the first and fourth quarters,” while Hog Production is “strongest in the second and third quarters.”
  • Steven France stated, “we're maintaining the call that we have for the outlook for the rest of the year, the $1.1 billion to $1.2 billion.”

Financial results

  • Hall reported consolidated sales of $3.8 billion and record adjusted operating profit of $339 million, with adjusted operating profit margin of 8.9%; he also reported adjusted net income of $251 million and adjusted diluted EPS of $0.64.
  • Hall attributed sales growth to “higher packaged meats and Mexico sales driven by strong volume growth,” partly offset by “a $155 million headwind from nonrecurring Hog Production sales to our joint venture partners in the prior year.”
  • By segment, Hall reported Packaged Meats operating profit of $275 million (12.8% margin), Fresh Pork operating profit of $78 million (3.9% margin), and Hog Production operating profit of $4 million; he said Other segment operating profit was $12 million, pressured by “softer sales and related losses in Bioscience that were partially offset by increases in Mexico.”
  • Hall said corporate expenses were “11% below the prior year,” and described liquidity and leverage as “$3.7 billion” and “0.4x,” respectively; he also said operating cash flow was a “net outflow of $65 million,” capex was $88 million, and the company paid a quarterly dividend of $0.3125 per share while expecting “$1.25 per share in annual dividends this year.”

Q&A

  • Peter Galbo, BofA Securities: Asked about guidance phasing; Chief Financial Officer Hall said Q2 Packaged Meats year-over-year profit cadence is affected by holiday timing and cited “higher-than-expected input inflation,” while expecting Fresh Pork “modestly up year-over-year” and Hog Production “a strong second quarter.”
  • Leah Jordan, Goldman Sachs: Asked about Packaged Meats competition and promotions; President of Packaged Meats France said, “We're not trying to manufacture volume through heavy promotions,” and described “quality versus unprofitable quantity,” while CEO Smith said, “I can't overemphasize enough the importance of the vertically integrated model now that it's working correctly.”
  • Megan Christine Alexander, Morgan Stanley: Pressed on Packaged Meats cost pressures and confidence within the outlook; Chief Financial Officer Hall said mitigation “will turn us back to that growth trajectory,” and President France said, “nothing has changed as far as how we feel about the long-term outlook of our Packaged Meats business,” while citing raw material costs “higher than last year by $94 million” and A&P spend “up 23% year-over-year.”
  • Alexander, Morgan Stanley: Asked about diesel and freight exposure; CEO Smith said “diesel cost is the biggest near-term impact,” and said the company took “about 1 million miles off the road” in 2025 vs. 2024 and has “line of sight to another 1 million miles” reduction in 2026 vs. 2025.
  • Benjamin Theurer, Barclays: Asked about grain costs and working capital; CEO Smith discussed feed procurement actions including “alternative ingredients” and “bakery byproducts,” and CFO Hall reiterated cash flow strength while noting first quarter seasonality.
  • Heather Jones, Heather Jones Research: Asked about pork-side raw material visibility amid disease; CEO Smith said “we're hearing the same things you are,” referencing “a higher incident rate of both PRRS and PEDV,” and Donovan Owens said the ASF situation in Spain has been “largely thus far a non-event” for U.S. demand.
  • Max Andrew Gumport, BNP Paribas: Asked about consumer sentiment and inflation; CFO Hall said, “we're not seeing a change that would require a material reset of our demand assumptions,” and said the company is “planning for volatility and staying agile.”
  • Saumya Jain, UBS: Asked about Packaged Meats vs. Fresh Pork demand and private label; President France said, “we don't see a trade down at all,” and Owens described Fresh Pork value-added gains, while France said private label expansion can benefit Smithfield’s capabilities.

Sentiment analysis

  • Analyst tone was slightly negative to neutral, concentrating on inflation pass-through (diesel, resin, beef/turkey), cadence risk, disease, and export demand; questions repeatedly tested whether new cost pressures changed guidance confidence.
  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more cautious in Q&A, repeatedly stressing mitigation levers and conservatism, including Hall’s “multiple levers we can pull” and France’s “planning the business with an appropriate level of conservatism.”
  • Versus the prior quarter’s emphasis on full-year transformation and large strategic initiatives, the current call’s tone leaned more toward near-term cost volatility and timing impacts while maintaining reiterated guidance language.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • The biggest new change from the prior call was timing: Smith said the Nathan’s Famous close is “now in the second half of 2026” due to CFIUS review delays tied to the partial government shutdown; in the prior quarter, management discussed the deal as pending without this revised closing window.
  • Discussion of Sioux Falls shifted from initiating approvals and construction timing in the prior quarter to the current-quarter message that the company will provide details “once we have secured final approvals.”
  • Analyst focus moved from broad 2026 setup (53rd week, segment guidance ranges, and Sioux Falls pacing) to more pointed Q2 phasing, transportation exposure, and whether Middle East-driven inflation altered 2026 assumptions.

Risks and concerns

  • Smith cited the Middle East conflict as “another layer of macro volatility,” flowing through “higher freight, packaging and agricultural input cost,” while Hall highlighted “near-term input and logistics cost uncertainty.”
  • Management cited continued “consumer caution,” higher input inflation (notably “beef and turkey”), and operational disruptions including “temporary winter storm disruptions” and lower China export volumes in Fresh Pork.
  • Disease and supply visibility were discussed as uncertain: Smith referenced higher PRRS and PEDV incidence and said “it's hard at this time of the year to have really clear visibility into what's out there.”

Final takeaway

Management described a record Q1 driven by Packaged Meats execution and cost discipline, reiterated full-year guidance language, and emphasized mitigation tools for higher freight, packaging, energy-linked logistics, and protein input volatility. The call’s most material update was the revised expectation that the Nathan’s Famous acquisition closes in the second half of 2026, while Sioux Falls remains subject to final approvals. Management maintained a cautious but steady stance on consumer demand and emphasized liquidity and low leverage as support for investment, dividends, and continued operational actions.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Telenor ASA reports Q1 results
data.description:
• Telenor ASA press release (TELNY): Q1 Revenue of NOK 14.8B.

    • Adjusted EBITDA of NOK 8.0 billion.

    • Free cash flow before M&A of NOK 2.1 billion.

    • Total free cash flow of 31.9 bn NOK.

    Service revenues increased by 1.6 per cent compared to the same period last year, while adjusted EBITDA grew by 3.1 per cent, both organically. The results reflect slower growth in the Nordics compared to 2025, especially in Finland, combined with a challenging situation in Bangladesh.
